planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full shade; hairy wood mint grows naturally in moist to mesic deciduous forest understory, shaded stream banks, and moist woodland margins throughout the eastern US.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade in woodland gardens, shaded borders, and moist shaded areas; hairy wood mint is a little-known native perennial of the mint family (Lamiaceae) with distinctively hairy stems and leaves and whorled clusters of small, two-lipped, white to pale lavender flowers with purple spotting in midsummer. The minty-aromatic foliage is pleasantly fragrant. An outstanding native for the challenging combination of shade and adequate moisture — one of the few mint-family plants truly adapted to woodland understory conditions. The flowers are visited extensively by small native bees, bumblebees, and butterflies. The leaves can be brewed as a mild mint tea with a pleasant oregano-mint blend flavor. Self-sows modestly in appropriate conditions to naturalize in the shade garden.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall for spring germination in well-drained, dry to moist, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or average soil in partial shade; downy wood mint is more drought-tolerant than hairy wood mint and grows in drier woodland conditions on rocky slopes, limestone outcrops, and dry open woodland throughout the eastern US.

directions: Plant in well-drained to dry, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline, average to rocky soil in partial shade to full sun; downy wood mint is a drought-tolerant native mint relative of dry, rocky, open woodlands, slopes, and wood margins across the eastern US, more tolerant of dry conditions than hairy wood mint and naturally at home on thin, rocky soils over limestone or sandstone. The whorled clusters of small, lavender to pale purple flowers with purple spotting are produced in midsummer and attract numerous small native bees, bumblebees, and clearwing sphinx moths. The leaves are fragrant with an oregano-mint scent and can be used as a culinary herb or brewed as tea. Spreads modestly by self-sowing in appropriate dry, rocky, shaded conditions. A refined, low-maintenance native herb for dry shade gardens, rocky woodland edges, and naturalistic settings in zones 4–8.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall or start indoors in late winter in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile to average soil in partial shade to full sun; purple bergamot is a naturally occurring hybrid between scarlet beebalm (M. didyma) and wild bergamot (M. fistulosa), combining traits of both parents.

directions: Plant in moist to average, fertile to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; purple bergamot is a natural hybrid monarda occurring where the ranges of scarlet beebalm (M. didyma) and wild bergamot (M. fistulosa) overlap, producing intermediate flowers in rich purple-lavender tones. The plant is more vigorous and mildew-resistant than pure M. didyma in many gardens and produces large, showy whorled flower heads that are exceptionally attractive to bumblebees, ruby-throated hummingbirds, and fritillary butterflies. The bergamot fragrance of the crushed leaves is distinctive and pleasant. Spreads by rhizomes to form substantial colonies in moist, fertile conditions — divide every 2–3 years to maintain vigor and control spread. An outstanding native pollinator plant that bridges the gap between the red and lavender monarda color spectrum in native plant gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; giant rush grows naturally in wet meadows, pond margins, and stream banks across Europe and western Asia. The cultivar 'Afro' has attractively spiraling and curling stems.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, average to fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; giant rush is a robust Eurasian rush producing tall, blue-green, somewhat rigid, cylindrical stems in dense clumps at the margins of wet areas. The blue-green color of the stems is more pronounced and attractive than most rushes, lending a cool, architectural quality to pond edges and rain gardens. The cultivar 'Afro' is widely available in the ornamental trade for its amusing, naturally spiraling and corkscrew-curling stems that maintain their twisted form throughout the season. The small, brown, starry flower clusters appear in mid-stem in summer. More vigorous and taller than soft rush (J. effusus), making it better suited to larger water features and naturalizing in extensive wet areas. Provides winter structure and shelter for wetland wildlife.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; knotted rush grows naturally in marshes, shallow pond margins, wet prairies, and stream banks across North America, particularly in the northern and central states where it grows in shallow standing water.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade in shallow pond margins, wet prairie gardens, and rain garden wet zones; knotted rush is a small native rush of northern and central North America, growing naturally in the shallow water and saturated soil margins of ponds, marshes, and wet meadows. The stems are distinctively knotted or nodose — with visible swellings or nodes along the stem — a feature that gives the plant its common name. The compact, rounded, dark brown flower and seed heads appear at mid-stem. A fine-textured native for freshwater wet habitats that provides seed food for marsh birds and shorebirds. Self-sows into appropriate wet conditions. Combines naturally with sedges, native wetland wildflowers, and other small rushes at the margins of naturalistic water gardens and pond edges in ecological plantings.
